title: Penn State softball upsets Northwestern, advances in Big Ten Tournament
author: Luci Palacios | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State opened postseason play on Wednesday against Northwestern in the first round of the Big Ten Tournament, looking to keep the season alive and punch a ticket to the next round.

The Nittany Lions advanced to the quarter finals with a 11-5 win over the Wildcats.

Northwestern turned to freshman right-hander Marina Mason in the circle to open tournament play. Mason immediately earned two strikeouts against Kaylie Walters and Allison Oneacre.

The Nittany Lions were left scoreless in the first as it was a quick three and out.

Sophomore right-hander Abigail Britton got the starting nod from the Nittany Lions. Northwestern’s Gracie Nieto worked a 10-pitch at bat against Britton and earned a walk to start off.

With two outs on the board, Nieto stole second and Kansas Robinson earned a walk. A wild pitch against Bridget Donahey allowed Robinson to steal second and Nieto to steal third.

Donahey proceeded to hit a three-run home run to put the Wildcats up 3-0 early in the game.

Nothing was working for the blue and white to start the second, as all they ended the half inning was a walk by Natalie Lieto and a hit by Frankee Flesher.

Northwestern came alive in the second as Isabel Cunnea started with a solo home run, putting the Wildcats up 4-0.

Coach Clarisa Crowell called for a pitching change, putting in freshman left-hander Brevyn Kellepouris, who proceeded to strikeout Teagan McCue to start the momentum for the Nittany Lions. Northwestern couldn’t get anything else going in the second.

The blue and white stepped it up in the third, as Gabby Gradishar opened it with an infield hit for a single.

Breanna Hanik stepped to the plate with two outs on the board and hit a two run home run, getting Penn State on the board 4-2 to end the third.

The Nittany Lions defense dominated in the third, allowing no Wildcat runs to cross home plate.

The Penn State offense’s dominance continued leading into the fourth, as Jordan George pinch hit for Flesher. George stepped to the plate and hit the ball towards third base to record a single.

After the next two outs, Gradishar hit another two run home run for Penn State, tying the game 4-4.

The Wildcats stepped it up in the bottom of the fourth as Tru Medina hit it to the outfield, almost getting a home run but ultimately settling for a double. Nieto followed up with a groundball up the middle to notch a single.

Kaylie Avvisato stepped to the plate and grounded out to Kellepouris. Despite the out, it allowed Medina to make it home, putting another Wildcat run on the board. Northwestern went back on top with a 5-4 lead against Penn State.

The blue and white fought back in the fifth, as Hanik hit it to left field for a single. Brooke Klosowicz struck out, but Hanik took an opportunity to steal second. It didn’t end there, as Lieto hit the Nittany Lions’ third two-run home run of the game to Penn State on top 6-5.

Most of Penn State’s runs at this point in the game came when they had two outs on the board.

The Nittany Lions switched pitchers again, this time going to McKenna Young to relieve Kellepouris.

Robinson led off the fifth with a double as pinch runner Kate Renschen stepped in for Robinson at second.

Donahey stepped up to the plate and hit a ball into the outfield, attempting to make it to second base, but a diving tag by Michela Barbanente saw her get out at second.

Northwestern was held to another scoreless inning.

The sixth inning was no different for the Nittany Lions, as Barbanente led off with a double. With a follow up two-run home run by Madison Seidel, the Penn State lead jumped to 8-5. This was the fourth home run of the game for the blue and white.

The Wildcats relieved Mason and put junior right-hander Renae Cunningham into the game

An action packed top of the sixth for the Nittany Lions as they proceeded to get 3 more runs, bringing up the lead 11-5.

Young put up a masterclass performance in the sixth and allowed no runs for Northwestern.

Momentum slowed down as the blue and white got no runs in the seventh, while Northwestern offered sophomore Signe Dohse onto the mound.

Young closed up the seventh with no runs, and Penn State defeated Northwestern.

Up Next

The Nittany Lions advance to the Big Ten quarterfinal, where they will go against No. 3 UCLA on Thursday at 7 p.m.

title: 3 questions Penn State needs to answer following spring ball
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State wrapped up the 15th practice of the spring on April 25 with Blue-White Practice, giving fans the first look at the Matt Campbell-led Nittany Lions.

In the week to follow, the team individually met with Campbell and their respective position coach, honing in on specific areas to grow over the summer months.

With the team entering an off period until team practice resumes in June, here’s a look at the biggest questions looming for Penn State following spring ball.

What’s the quarterback depth behind Rocco Becht?

Heading into spring, Iowa State transfer Rocco Becht was without a doubt going to be the starter in Week 1, followed by fellow former Cyclone Alex Manske.

While Manske has been away from the team recovering from an injury, Christopher Newport transfer Connor Barry has made a name for himself, drawing a lot of praise from Campbell.

“Connor Barry, he’s come in, and he’s really done a great job, probably been the surprise so far, of four days of practice, just his ability to get quality reps,” Campbell said on March 31.

However, Barry’s Penn State debut at Blue-White Practice warranted mixed reactions. The senior quarterback threw a passing touchdown and rushed for a score, but also threw an interception.

Despite the underwhelming performance, Barry has put himself in a position to battle Manske for the No. 2 spot, a contest that will go deep into summer.

Outside of Becht, Manske and Barry, none of the other three quarterbacks had great showings at the spring practice. Jack Lambert has yet to see any playing time despite three years in the program, while freshmen Kase Evans and Peyton Falzone showed youthful struggles.

Who will cement themselves on the defensive line?

Penn State’s defensive end and defensive tackle rooms boast very different experience levels. The defensive end room has mostly younger players, while veteran players highlight the defensive tackles.

Despite this, both starting positions are still up in the air as players recover from injury and others continue to show their potential at practice.

Yvan Kemajou and Max Granville — both returners from last year’s squad — have emerged as two potential starters at defensive end. Kemajou burned his redshirt last season and was one of the top freshmen on the team, while Granville missed the entirety of 2025 with an injury.

However, Colorado transfer Alex McPherson and Iowa State transfer Ike Ezeogu, who started 11 games last year, are also in the mix.

At defensive tackle, defensive line coach Ikaika Malloe brought a pair of veterans in Siale Taupaki and Keanu Williams with him from UCLA, while Oklahoma State transfer Armstrong Nnodim and Utah transfer Dallas Vakalahi have also been names to watch.

Despite being the youngest of the bunch as just a sophomore, Nnodim has drawn praise from Campbell and other players on the team as a strong competitor throughout spring practice.

Will Penn State be fully healthy Week 1?

The Nittany Lions were missing several pieces of their potential starting lineup this spring due to ongoing injuries. Becht was limited along with wide receivers Chase Sowell and Brett Eskildsen.

Meanwhile, Granville, tight end Andrew Rappleyea, starting offensive lineman Anthony Donkoh, center Dom Rulli and linebackers Tony Rojas and Alex Tatsch are expected to be at full strength at the start of the season, barring any setbacks.

Penn State has struggled at wide receiver in recent years, and with Sowell and Eskildsen recovering during spring, fans have yet to see if those struggles will continue again with a different starting squad.

Along the offensive line, Anthony Donkoh missed spring practice following an unspecified medical procedure. He’s expected to hit the starting lineups when he returns, likely for the start of the season.

On defense, Rojas is one of the biggest names who’s yet to see the field this spring, as he tore his ACL during practice last season ahead of Penn State’s game against UCLA.

When he returns to the field, Rojas will likely start for the third straight season. He’s on track to return for Week 1, but was sidelined during all of spring ball.

During Rojas’ absence in the fall, then-freshman Alex Tatsch stepped up and played in six games. However, he too missed spring practice to injury, and will look for a role in the linebacker room when healthy.

Granville is another big name who’s looking to grace the starting lineup. He’s yet to establish himself as a top defender on Penn State’s defense, but with Dani Dennis-Sutton, Zuriah Fisher and Chaz Coleman gone, he has the opportunity to step up and be a starter if he’s healthy.

title: Penn State Athletics announces 2026 football student ticket information
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State Athletics has announced the initial information for student football tickets for the 2026 season.

The system will run as a lottery, with the seven-game ticket package totaling $246, the same price and system as the past two years.

The ticket window opens on July 20, with the graduate students' request window. Those students will have until Monday, July 27, to request tickets.

The senior ticket window opens at 9 a.m. on Tuesday, July 21, and closes at 5 p.m. on July 28, giving each class a week to submit for tickets. Juniors can begin requesting tickets at 9 a.m. on July 22 and close one week later at 5 p.m. on July 29.

title: Penn State women’s lacrosse set for NCAA Tournament matchup against Stanford
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Three hundred and fifty two days after being named the 10th head coach in Penn State history, Kayla Treanor and the Nittany Lions will compete in the NCAA tournament.

Penn State will face Stanford at 6 p.m. on Friday in the first round of the tournament, marking its first appearance since 2024.

While the two teams have only faced off twice in program history, the Nittany Lions will look to earn their first NCAA tournament victory since 2017 on Friday.

All-time matchups

The two squads faced off in a home-and-home series in 2015 and 2016, each claiming a victory.

The Nittany Lions opened with a 17-15 victory in 2015, upsetting No. 9 Stanford. Penn State went on a 10-3 run starting with three minutes left to ultimately win the game with four hat tricks.

Things went a little differently the second time around, as No. 8 Penn State fell 16-10 to the Cardinal on the road, being upset in enemy territory.

While the blue and white never led, an 8-1 run spanning between the second and third quarter put the nail in the coffin as Penn State fell by six goals to even the series.

Penn State’s tournament history

Penn State has made the NCAA tournament 27 times in program history, advancing past its first game just 13 times.

The Nittany Lions have struggled in recent tournament appearances, falling in the first round three times in the past eight years. Outside of those faults, Penn State was last successful in 2017, when it earned a spot in the Final Four for the second year in a row.

While the blue and white has yet to make it to the big game in the 21st century, it had lots of success from joining the NCAA in 1983 to the late 1990s.

Penn State earned nine Final Four appearances in 16 years, emerging victorious two times.

The Nittany Lions won NCAA championships in 1987 and 1989, defeating Temple and Harvard, respectively.

2026 Cardinal

Stanford had a dominant start to the season, winning 10 straight games.

Through the first two months of action, the Cardinal earned five top-25 victories, including wins against No. 9 Clemson, No. 13 Syracuse, No. 21 Virginia, No. 22 Duke and No. 11 Notre Dame.

After falling to Boston College and getting upset by Villanova, Stanford bounced back with a 17-goal victory against Louisville before losing its third game of the season to North Carolina, which it also lost to in the season finale and ACC title game.

The Cardinal have put up the fifth-most goals of any team in the nation this year, scoring 272 goals through 19 contests.

However, no player emerged as the frontrunner in scoring this season. Junior Ava Arceri leads the team with 46 goals, followed by redshirt junior Aliya Polisky with 44 goals.

In addition to the high-scoring offense, Stanford has been led by a pair of dominant goalkeepers in Lucy Pearson and Ella Morton, who have shared the net this year.

Pearson started the first nine games of the season before missing seven games due to injury. She returned for the last three games of the year, posting a 43.4% save rate on the season. In her absence, Morton posted a 43.8% save record in her seven starts.

Friday’s matchup

It will be over two weeks between Penn State’s Big Ten quarterfinal game against Michigan and the NCAA first round contest on Friday, giving plenty of time for rest for Penn State.

The Nittany Lions are coming off a successful first regular season for Treanor, winning 12 games — three of which were against top-25 opponents.

Outside of freshman phenom Rocquette Allen, who was named Big Ten Freshman of the Year last week, Penn State sought production from Kelly MacKinney and Payton Wainman, who all had over 40 goals on the year.

Penn State and Stanford rank as the 15th and 16th-highest scoring offenses in the country, averaging 14.56 and 14.32 points per game.

Friday’s game will be a high-scoring affair, but will likely be largely affected by draw controls, an area where Penn State’s struggled this year. The Nittany Lions have won just 47.9% of their draw controls, while Stanford has won 52.2%.

The Cardinal are coming off a first round victory in last year’s tournament and will look to carry that into this year’s performance, while Penn State looks for its first victory since 2017.

title: Penn State Athletics announces death of Director of Athletic Medicine Wayne Sebastianelli
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State Athletics announced longtime Director of Athletic Medicine Wayne Sebastianelli has died.

Sebastianelli served as the director of athletic medicine for 30 years, working in the field for 38 years total.

"Dr. Sebastianelli embodied the very best of Penn State," Vice President of Intercollegiate Athletics Pat Kraft said in the release. "He cared deeply for our student-athletes as individuals, always prioritizing their health, safety and well-being above all else."

While serving as the director of athletic medicine at Penn State, Sebastianelli also served as the associate chief medical officer for Penn State Health, Centre County Region, since 2016.

Sebastianelli was on the Big Ten Sports Medicine subcommittee, while also serving as the chief of staff at Mount Nittany Medical Center and Mount Nittany Surgical Center for 16 years.

"The impact he had on this football program and athletics department was second to none," Matt Campbell said in the release. "Penn State football will be forever grateful for his contributions and the standard he set for everyone around him."

title: Penn State softball set to take on Northwestern in opening round of Big Ten Tournament
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
After going 32-20 in the regular season with an 11-13 record in Big Ten play, Penn State has earned the right to play in the postseason. The Nittany Lions are slated as the No.11-seed in the 12-team Big Ten Tournament.

The blue and white drew a redemption matchup in the first round, as they’re set to play sixth-seeded Northwestern. The Wildcats swept Penn State in their three-game series at Beard Field a little under a month ago and finished conference play 16-8

The Nittany Lions will take on Northwestern at 7 p.m. on Wednesday at Maryland Softball Stadium.

The Wildcats took two of three games against Minnesota last weekend. The blue and white saw an opposite result after losing all three meetings with No. 3 Nebraska.

Penn State was overwhelmed the first time the squads faced, with the Wildcats outscoring the Nittany Lions by a combined 38-9 margin across all three games.

Freshman pitcher Marina Mason was lights out in the circle for Northwestern in its regular season meeting with Penn State. The Brentwood, Tennessee, native pitched 11 ⅓ innings against the blue and white, combining for 15 strikeouts while allowing just 5 hits.

Mason has been forceful in the circle all year for the Wildcats, owning the second-lowest ERA in the conference at 2.12. In Northwestern’s season-finale against Minnesota on Sunday, Mason recorded her seventh complete-game shutout of the season, ranking fifth nationally and leading all Big Ten pitchers. Additionally, the freshman tossed a season-high 12 strikeouts while allowing just one hit.

As of late, the most productive arm in Penn State’s pitching staff has been junior right-hander McKenna Young, who owns a team-best 3.60 ERA across 74 innings pitched. Young pitched 3 ⅓ innings across two appearances against the Wildcats, surrendering eight hits and seven earned runs.

Bridget Nemeth had a strong outing in her most recent start against No. 3 Nebraska on Saturday. The right-hander pitched 5 ⅓ innings, allowing just three hits, three earned runs and struck out five.

Offensively, Northwestern is led by junior Emma Raye, whose .394 batting-average and 57 RBIs both rank top-15 in the Big Ten. Raye also leads the Wildcats with 14 home runs and a .789 slugging-percentage. Against Penn State, Raye went a combined 9-for-11, with six RBIs.

Kansas Robinson and Izzy Cunnea had strong outings in the Wildcats’ last time out against Minnesota, each going 1-for-3 and combined for four of the team's six RBIs.

Penn State’s offense is paced by freshman Allison Oneacre’s .380 batting-average, also her 61 RBIs and 18 home runs are both top-10 in the conference. Oneacre struggled against the Wildcats in the regular season, recording just two hits and two RBIs across three games.

Breanna Hanik had a strong weekend against the Cornhuskers, recording a multi-hit performance in all three contests. The freshman has been the Nittany Lions’ best bat in conference play, batting .405 in this regard.

As Penn State looks for redemption, Northwestern will attempt to complete the full season sweep of the Nittany Lions. A date with third-seed UCLA awaits the winner in the quarterfinals.

title: Residents express concerns about housing affordability during borough council meeting
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
Several public comments were made to the State College Borough Council during their meeting on Monday regarding concerns of housing affordability within the borough.

The comments began when Kathryn Santoro, a resident of Addison Court who has made previous public comments about her inability to find housing in the face of the building’s potential sale and redevelopment, said she has not been able to find an apartment with only one bedroom that is able to support her disability.

Santoro requires a wheelchair to move from place to place.

“I picked Addison for its convenient access to community libraries, entertainment and buses,” Santoro said. “Other apartments do not have these conveniences and they get very difficult to be independent.”

Santoro said she has been told by realtors that there is a six to nine month waitlist, and one apartment told her there wasn’t going to be any availability until 2027.

Santoro said she also found it hard to believe that county and local officials didn’t know about the expiration of Addison Court’s 30-year lease or try to stop the sale.

During her public comment, Santoro delivered a letter with 148 signatures, some coming from Penn State students who spoke to her about their rent payments.

“Students tell me that those doing one bedroom apartments for $1,200, those condos, they're paying $1,200 a week and landlords are telling them they're gonna have to double up 'cause they want to charge more money,” Santoro said. “Is there a code that they can have 8 unrelated people in an apartment? We used to have a code that you couldn't have 3 unrelated people in housing.”

State College resident Cynthia Carpenter posited to the council the potential of instituting a tax on the ownership of second homes as a way to combat housing unaffordability in the borough, since she read about the concept in a Wall Street Journal article.

She also said the borough could explore instituting a small tax whose payments would go towards funding affordable housing. Her daughter lives in London and told her about a similar program there.

“It's a beautiful neighborhood, but they've been able to put affordable housing and mix it throughout the city,” Carpenter said.

Johan Zwart, another State College resident, said he felt the downtown area was now catering more towards developers than longtime residents such as himself.

“I hear people talk here about sustainable housing and about low income housing, but it's all talk,” Zwart said. “In the same time that people say that, we tear down the low-income housing for another high rise, or another mall or another whatsoever but the people who live there, they just move them out and they're silenced. They're gone.”

Zwart concluded his public comment by expressing what he saw as a “lack of anticipation” from previous council administrations to prevent situations where residents are pushed out of the downtown area to make way for new developments. He brought up Addison Court as a current example of the council’s inaction.

Following these public comments, Council Member John Hayes said, while he and other council members may agree with many of the comments presented, there are legal constraints. State College has to abide by as a home rule municipality that prevents them from considering many of the ideas proposed.

This caused Santoro to speak up on what she believed the borough could do.

“My understanding is board members, planning commissioners, plan for these high rises, for these apartments,” Santoro said. “You have the power to say, ‘no, we can't have these high rises.’ This needs to stop. The rent is astronomical.”

title: Evan Myers, Mayor Nanes make statements regarding accusations from ICE protest, promise ICE ordinance
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
Council President Evan Myers and State College Mayor Ezra Nanes began Monday’s borough council meeting with statements responding to accusations made during an April 20 protest against ICE.

The protest, which was organized by the People’s Defense Front and Student Committee for Defense and Solidarity, was held in response to alleged State College Police Department (SCPD) involvement in an ICE arrest on March 25, as well as the borough’s lack of an ordinance protecting residents from ICE operations.

During the protest, flyers were distributed accusing Myers, Nanes and SCPD Chief Joseph Merrill of aiding and abetting “ICE terror.” A banner was also unfurled by protestors inside the council chamber with a similar message.

Myers said the borough had investigated if SCPD was actively involved during the arrest, and he said the findings showed no evidence of such involvement. He also said the borough is working on a new ICE ordinance proposal, which will be discussed during the council’s July 6 meeting.

“If any police department was involved, it was not State College police,” Myers said. “I understand and appreciate the desire to put pressure on us to act, and act, we must. We will continue to do what we can and will look at ways to take more action than just words.”

Myers also called out the role of House Rep. Glenn Thompson and Senator Dave McCormick in continuing to vote for ICE funding.

“Thompson has criticized this council for taking the actions that already have gone to protect our residents and oppose ICE,” Myers said. “We stand on the side of those residents. We stand on the side of immigrants, and we know what side we're on.”

Nanes said he strongly agrees with the council’s decision to protect “the dignity, the safety and the rights” of all those living in the borough and stated his commitment to “hearing and understanding the views of our community.”

“Public comment, advocacy and protest are essential parts of our local democracy,” Nanes said. “This meeting here is also a place where we must be able to conduct the public's business with respect for one another and for the community that we serve.”

Nanes also called attention to the accusations made against him and others during the April 20 protest and stated his continued commitment to protecting the State College community through “lawful, meaningful action.”

“I do not intend in this moment to give further life to false statements that were made about me or about other people, but I will say this: it is painful to see oneself publicly portrayed in a way that does not reflect the truth of one's words, actions, values or record,” Nanes said. “False claims about public officials are not harmless. They can cause injury far beyond what is easily seen in the context of a public meeting. They can deepen division in the community they are trying to serve and make it harder to do the urgent, careful and necessary work that is before us.”

During public comments later in the meeting, State College resident Nicholas Dudek — who also expressed concerns about ICE activity in the borough during the April 20 meeting — said, while he appreciated that the council is still considering an ICE ordinance, he is concerned about some council members believing not passing an ordinance will let State College fly under the radar.

“In a moment like this, we can't turn a blind eye and just keep our heads down,” Dudek said. “Whether it's now or whether it's later, it will be our turn to be targeted.”

Dudek said continued inaction by the council on this issue will ultimately lead to the same consequences as actively aiding ICE operations. He urged the borough to look at examples of ICE ordinances currently implemented across Pennsylvania, such as Philadelphia’s “ICE OUT” legislation.

“These are actual doable, actionable items that create meaningful safety for our community and they have been taken by others,” Dudek said. “There's no reason that we can't follow in their footsteps.”

author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is going dancing in Year 1 under coach Kayla Treanor.

The Nittany Lions earned a bid to the 2026 NCAA Tournament during the selection show on Sunday, entering the tournament as an at-large team.

𝐆𝐎𝐈𝐍𝐆 𝐃𝐀𝐍𝐂𝐈𝐍𝐆 𝐈𝐍 𝐀𝐍𝐍 𝐀𝐑𝐁𝐎𝐑Penn State. Stanford. Friday. 6 PM pic.twitter.com/oZrVsvxot3— Penn State Women’s Lacrosse (@PennStateWLAX) May 4, 2026

Penn State will face Stanford in the first round of the NCAA Tournament, facing off at 6 p.m. on Friday.

Penn State last earned a spot in the NCAA Tournament in 2024, when it fell to James Madison in the first round. The Nittany Lions haven’t advanced past the first round since 2017, when they fell to Maryland in the Final Four.

Treanor is no stranger to the NCAA Tournament, having taken Syracuse to four straight tournament appearances in her four years up north.

During the 2023 and 2024 season, Treanor guided the Orange to back-to-back Final Four appearances as the No. 3 seed, falling to No. 2 Boston College each year.

In her first year at Penn State, the Nittany Lions opened the season 9-0, marking their best start since 1989. During the same game, Penn State became the third team in NCAA history to win 600 or more games.

Despite the strong start, Penn State began to struggle against higher-ranked Big Ten opponents, losing three straight to Princeton, Michigan and Maryland.

After winning two straight Ohio State and Oregon, the Nittany Lions lost another two games before closing out the season with a ranked victory at Rutgers.

Penn State opened the postseason with a Big Ten quarterfinals matchup against Michigan, falling 13-12. The Nittany Lions look to continue Treanor’s first year in the NCAA Tournament.

title: Penn State softball can’t stop the long ball, falls to No. 3 Nebraska on Senior Day
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State welcomed its highest attendance of the season in Saturday’s doubleheader against No. 3 Nebraska. The fans showed out again on Sunday for the Nittany Lions Senior Day and regular-season finale.

Penn State fell to the Cornhuskers 13-5 in an offensive battle.

The blue and white started the afternoon honoring its three seniors, catchers Madison Seidel and Jordyn Jochims who’ve been with Penn State for four years, along with graduate outfielder Cara Bohner who joined the Nittany Lions this season.

Freshman left-hander Brevyn Kellepouris started things off in the circle for Penn State. Communication issues on the right side of the infield allowed Jordy Frahm and Hannah Coor to reach on infield singles.

With two baserunners, Hannah Camenzind grounded a ball back to Kellepouris, but the early infield woes continued. Kellepouris attempted to throw out the lead runner, but, under threw third baseman Allison Oneacre, allowing Frahm to come around and score for the first run of the game.

The Cornhuskers added two more runs on a double from Ava Kuszak, but Kellepouris settled in for the remainder of the inning.

Junior right-hander Kylee Magee got the nod for Nebraska, surrendering walks to Kaylie Walters and Brooke Klosowicz, but otherwise kept the Nittany Lions’ bats silent.

The leash was short for Kellepouris as coach Clarisa Crowell made a pitching change in the second frame, bringing in sophomore right-hander Abigail Britton.

Britton allowed two baserunners, but was backed by her defense, denying the Cornhuskers a chance to extend their lead.

Michela Barbanente led off the second inning with Penn State’s first hit of the day. The second baseman traded her spot on first base with Natalie Lieto after Magee opted to go to second on a fielder's choice.

Magee allowed another baserunner after hitting Frankee Flesher with a pitch, but a fly out from Breanna Hanik ended the Nittany Lions’ threat.

A leadoff home run in the top of the third inning from Jesse Farrell extended Nebraska’s lead to 4-0. The Cornhuskers threat continued as a couple of singles and a fielding error from Penn State first baseman Gabby Gradishar allowed a bases loaded, no outs situation.

Lauren Camenzind knocked a double on a full count, bringing in two more Nebraska runs and forcing the Nittany Lions to make another pitching change.

Junior right-hander McKenna Young entered the circle with a tall task of facing the top of the Cornhuskers order.

A sacrifice fly from Frahm and an RBI single from Coor, brought in two more runs for Nebraska as the Cornhuskers led 8-0 after their five-run third inning.

Penn State welcomed its two, three and four hitters to the plate in the home half of the third, in dire need of finding a way onto the scoreboard. The Nittany Lions did just that.

Walters led off the inning with a single, followed by a double from Oneacre. Klosowicz stepped into the box, in what was Penn State’s best scoring opportunity of the day. The junior came through for the Nittany Lions, knocking a three-run home run for her first hit of the weekend.

The Nebraska defense stopped the Nittany Lions’ run count at three, still holding onto a five-run lead entering the fourth frame.

A double from Farrell gave the Cornhuskers a run back in the top of the inning, extending their lead to 9-3.

Nebraska made a pitching change in the bottom of the fourth inning, bringing in freshman left-hander Alexis Jensen.

Penn State quickly put runners on the corners with one out as Jordan George came to the plate to pinch hit. George knocked a sacrifice fly, scoring Seidel from third and putting the Nittany Lions deficit back at five runs.

Junior right-hander Bridget Nemeth entered the circle in the fifth inning as the blue and white’s fourth inning of the game.

Frahm launched a home run for the Cornhuskers to keep their cushion intact, but Nemeth otherwise kept the Nebraska bats quiet.

Penn State saw two baserunners in the bottom of the fifth inning, but couldn’t find a way to bring them home.

Nemeth surrendered another home run in the top of the sixth frame, this time a two-run shot off the bat of Jensen. This long ball put Nebraska in run rule territory, leading 12-4, forcing Penn State to have to score at least one run to keep the game alive.

The blue and white found a way to extend the game thanks to a two-out solo home run from Breanna Hanik.

Freshman right-hander Mackenzie Duncan entered the circle in the seventh as the final available arm for Penn State.

It didn’t take long for Nebraska to jump on Duncan, as Frahm launched a home run on the first pitch she saw for her second, and the team's fourth long ball of the game.

The Nittany Lions entered their final at-bats welcoming their heart of the order to the plate, down 13-5.

The blue and white came up empty, falling down in order.

Up Next

Penn State awaits their placement and opponent for the Big Ten Tournament, which kicks off in College Park, Maryland, on Wednesday.

title: Penn State baseball closes Dollar Dog Weekend with win over Minnesota
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
After losing both legs of its doubleheader with Minnesota on Saturday, Penn State wrapped up the three-game series with a Sunday matinee at Medlar Field at Lubrano Park.

The blue and white grinded out a close, 4-3 victory to avoid the sweep and cap off the weekend with a victory.

Nittany Lion pitcher Isaiah Shayter worked to get three consecutive Golden Gophers out in the top of the first inning, with all three flyouts being caught by senior outfielder Maddox McDonald.

Two runners reached base for the blue and white in the bottom of the frame, but neither could do any damage on the scoreboard. Shayter responded with a scoreless frame of his own to bring Penn State up to bat in the bottom of the second.

Both teams’ bats started to wake up a bit, trading deep fly balls, but none of them found the turf or the far side of the wall and the game remained scoreless into the third inning.

Minnesota infielder Jack Mosh ripped a grounder down the first base line that flirted with the chalk, but stayed fair and slipped into the outfield. Mosh settled in at second base, but a flyout and lineout to right field by his ensuing teammates brought the top of the third inning to a close.

Freshman infielder Preston Yaucher hit a dribbler down the third base line in the bottom of the inning that seemingly was destined to roll foul, but nicked the base to stay fair. A strong single by leadoff hitter Jayden Davis pushed Yaucher to third base with no outs in the inning.

A wild pitch from Minnesota’s Cole Selvig cruised towards the Penn State dugout as Yaucher eased across home plate to make the score 1-0 in favor of the blue and white. Davis advanced to third on a groundout by second baseman Bryce Molinaro, but a flyout by Kevin Karstetter stranded him.

Weber Neels flew out to open the top of the fourth inning before a Charlie Suth single gave the Golden Gophers a baserunner. Ty Allen grounded one to Spencer Barnett, who fired it to second, starting a slick double play that saw Penn State retire the side.

Barnett walked to start the bottom of the fourth, advanced on a sacrifice bunt and nearly stole third base, but was returned to second base on an interference call. A hit by Jesse Jaconski gave Barnett the chance to come home, but a powerful throw from Minnesota centerfielder Easton Richter saw him put out on a close play at the plate.

The ballgame was tied early in the top of the fifth, when Richter followed up on his play in the field with a smooth stroke that sent a ball just over the wall in left field. A strikeout and groundout nearly stopped the damage there, but Mosh mashed one into the right-field gap to keep the game alive with a double.

Davis Hamilton got a hold of a Shayter pitch, but Jaconski made the catch in right field to usher in a race of three hot dog-costumed fans, which was narrowly won by the ketchup-dressed frank.

Gambino called on Mason Horwat to take the mound in the top of the sixth. He opened with a groundout before allowing his first hit on a strong grounder from Suth that slipped past Yaucher and into the outfield. Two quick outs stranded the runner at first.

Karstetter delivered a leadoff single, but two consecutive Penn State batters were retired to bring Jaconski up to the plate. The senior right fielder crushed one to deep center field, slamming off the batter’s eye for a two-run home run to give the Nittany Lions a 3-1 lead in the bottom of the sixth.

Following a single by McDonald, a blooper to first base by Yaucher found the grass and drove in another run to extend the lead. Yaucher made an attempt to steal second base, and the throw from Neels behind the dish sailed into the outfield, allowing the Penn State shortstop to cruise into third.

With runners on second and third, a single from Spanier at the top of the seventh brought in a Minnesota run, cutting the Nittany Lion lead to two. Two walks and a wild pitch loaded the bases with two away in the frame, but a flyout stranded all three runners.

Penn State threatened again in the bottom of the frame with back-to-back singles, but a late steal attempt by Karstetter with two away sent the game to the eighth inning.

Richter continued to put the Golden Gophers on his back, sending a line drive over the high wall in right field for his second round-tripper of the game, bringing the Penn State advantage to just 4-3.

Harrison Lollin took the mound for Penn State in the top of the ninth, looking to close out the series. A leadoff walk to Mosh and a high pitch from Lollin put a runner in scoring position with no outs in the inning, and a bunt from Spanier moved him to third base.

With Mosh threatening, a flyout brought Neels up to bat with two outs. The Golden Gopher worked the count full, but fouled out to seal the victory for the Nittany Lions.

Up Next

Penn State will stay in Happy Valley for a three-game series with Washington, kicking off at 5:30 p.m. Friday.

title: Penn State softball shows offensive adjustments in Game 2 but falls twice to No. 3 Nebraska
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Due to a rainy Friday night in Happy Valley, Penn State was forced to prepare to take on No. 3 Nebraska twice in one day.

The Nittany Lions ultimately dropped both games of their Saturday doubleheader, struggling to come away with a win despite keeping the door open for themselves throughout the day.

“We for the most part, for two games, we were right there with them today,” coach Clarisa Crowell said. “So we feel good about the things that we did well.”

What the blue and white did well was make evident improvements from its quiet Game 1 offensive performance, showing more life and consistency at the plate in its second go at Nebraska.

“I thought we just had a great game plan and executed at a high level offensively,” Crowell said.

Facing the Cornhuskers ace and Big Ten ERA leader Jordy Frahm to start the day, Penn State knocked just four hits in its 5-1 opening loss, finding it difficult to generate sustained offense early on.

“She's one of the best pitchers in the country,” Crowell said. “And to hit against a pitcher like that, when she throws you a good pitch to hit, you have to barrel it up.”

The numbers noticeably upgraded in Game 2 as the lineup settled, this time against Nebraska’s number two arm Alexis Jensen. Despite losing by a larger 10-4 margin, the Nittany Lions managed to score four earned runs and powered eight hits.

Entering Saturday’s two-game slate, Nebraska owned a 1.94 ERA, a testament to the strength and depth of its pitching staff. This mark is not only the best in the Big Ten, but it also ranks among the top five nationally.

Jensen, a freshman left-hander, possesses a top-six ERA in the conference at 2.57. Penn State’s seven hits against the Gretna, Nebraska, native are tied for the second most she’s allowed in a single-game all season.

“Jensen, that young lady's a very good pitcher, and I thought we put quality at bats together,” Crowell said. “Against that pitching staff, our offense did what they needed to do.”

A lot of the blue and white’s success in the early portions of its second game was a combination of both strong offense and dominance from junior right-hander Bridget Nemeth in the circle.

The Glendora, California native, held the Cornhuskers, one of the Big Ten’s best offenses, hitless through their first five plate appearances.

“That was Bridget of old today, I thought she pitched really, really well,” Crowell said. “She pitched well enough for us to win the game and that's a great team that she was able to shut down over there.”

Nemeth’s strong start was backed by her offense as Penn State led 4-1 after five innings. However, as Nebraska made its adjustments the Nittany Lions couldn’t keep their momentum and consistency.

Frahm entered the circle for the last two innings for the Cornhusksers, allowing one hit, no walks and striking out three.

“I thought we went five innings strong,” Crowell said. “But, we’ve got to figure out a way to go seven innings strong.”

title: Penn State baseball falls to Minnesota in second game of Saturday doubleheader
author: Ava Aguiar | The Daily Collegian
description:
After a close loss in the first game of its doubleheader against Minnesota, Penn State returned to the field looking for redemption against the Golden Gophers.

The blue and white fell 5-3 to Minnesota, marking their fifth consecutive loss in the last week, and another chance at a critical series win gone.

“It comes down to not executing fundamentals in one spot each game. Sometimes those are things that open up games and sometimes they’re close games, which is for me not okay, not acceptable, it’s not the standard we’ve set here. We’re not to the level we want right now,” said coach Mike Gambino.

Who else but senior Michael Anderson got it going for the Nittany Lions in the bottom of the first, launching his 19th homer of the season into left field. A quick turnover to the Golden Gophers saw Penn State back up to bat.

After a quiet two innings, Penn State put up another run after two singles by Jayden Davis and Anderson put them on first and third. Davis was able to score on a walk to first by Molinaro to put the team up 2-0, before two outs ended the inning.

Nittany Lion pitcher Ben Hudson kept Minnesota off the bases in the first three innings until Jack Spanier singled to right field before advancing to second on a hit-by-pitch on Weber Neels. Hudson earned his second strikeout of the game against Charlie Sutherland, before another strikeout on Ty Allen marked another inning off the board for the Golden Gophers.

It didn’t last long, as Minnesota’s Easton Richter’s strong plays in the top of the fifth saw Hudson begin to struggle. Richter started out the inning with a double down the left field line before a wild pitch left him waiting on third. Jack Bello was able to send him home off a single to right field that gave Minnesota their first run of the game.

After Hudson hit his second batter of the day, the bases were left loaded with maroon and gold, with two outs on the board. Neels launched a ball into center field, getting him to second base, and letting in all three batters for the Golden Gophers to take back the lead 4-2. Kyle Emmons came in to relieve Hudson after his solid five inning run.

Minnesota also saw its first pitching switch up with Tyler Hemmes coming in for Marcus Kruza. Hemmes quickly took out the Penn State offense, before Minnesota was back up to bat. Brayden Hellum was left stranded on second base as the rest of the hitting line up was taken out by Emmons.

Both teams remained scoreless through the top of the seventh, before an action packed bottom of the inning for Joey DeMucci. Coming in for Hemmes, Minnesota pitcher Will Whelan slammed DeMucci with a hard hit to the kneecap, leaving him on the ground. DeMucci eventually was able to limp his way down the first base line, and shake it off. He closed out the bottom of the seventh with a score off a wild pitch to put Penn State behind by one, going into the eighth at 5-3.

“He’s a tough kid because that thing hurt, 94 off the kneecap and then to go basically score a run by himself, he’s a tough competitive kid,” Gambino said.

A back and forth game, brought it all down to the last two innings. Minnesota’s Neel scored the team their fifth run of the game with no outs on the board in the top of the eighth. Two outs by Richter and Hellum left it up to Bello, where he came up just short of a homer to turn it back over to Penn State.

Another inning gone for the blue and white, staying two runs behind Minnesota going into the ninth. Dimond Loosli came onto the mound for Emmons, looking to prevent the Golden Gophers from furthering the gap.

Loosli did the trick, giving the Nittany Lions one last chance at a comeback. Penn State came up just short, falling to the Golden Gophers 5-3.

“We just gotta be able to push,keep getting better with that and then take our lessons for the young guys as we’re building this thing. Sometimes you just have to touch the stove,” Gambino said.

Up Next

Penn State will return home to Medlar Field at Lubrano Park to close out their series against Minnesota on Sunday at 11:30 a.m.

title: Penn State men's lacrosse's Hunter Aquino highlights Big Ten All-Tournament Team selections
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
After defeating Johns Hopkins 16-8 on Saturday to win the Big Ten Tournament, Penn State has earned plenty of names on the All-Tournament Team.

Hunter Aquino was named the tournament's most outstanding player with five goals and two assists en route to the Nittany Lions' second-ever title. He missed five games of his sophomore year with an injury, but Aquino returned at the right time, fueling Penn State to a 3-1 record in its last four games.

Your 2026 @B1GLacrosse Tournament Most Outstanding Player: Hunter Aquino 🫡 https://t.co/z7BMAgQe76 pic.twitter.com/E15uSCUvbY— Penn State Men’s Lacrosse (@PennStateMLAX) May 2, 2026

Alex Ross, Colby Baldwin, Kyle Lehman and Preston Hawkins were the other Nittany Lions to earn All-Tournament honors, as all four played a key role in Penn State's success in the Big Ten Tournament.

Big Ten All-Tournament Team honors for this group* 👏*not pictured: Hunter Aquino pic.twitter.com/LFufaCun5U— Penn State Men’s Lacrosse (@PennStateMLAX) May 2, 2026

Ross had three ground balls in the two tournament games, showcasing why the New York Atlas decided to draft him eighth overall. Baldwin, a faceoff specialist, had nine ground balls against Maryland and the Blue Jays while winning 65.4% of faceoffs.

Lehman had three goals, four ground balls and one caused turnover in Penn State's title run, while Hawkins, a true freshman, was a sturdy presence in net, allowing just 14 goals across the tournament.

title: Penn State softball falls to No. 3 Nebraska in late-inning collapse
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
In Penn State’s first game of Saturday's doubleheader, No.3 Nebraska took the first win of the day. The blue and white took on the Cornhuskers for their second game of the series Saturday afternoon.

The Nittany Lions got ahead of No.3 Nebraska but were unable to hold the lead falling 10-4.

Head Coach Matt Campbell started things off with the first pitch, his first ceremonial pitch so far in his time in Happy Valley.

Junior right hander Bridget Nemeth got the start in the circle for the blue and white. Starting off strong, Nemeth had a quick inning getting her first two strikeouts of the game against. With one of her strikeouts being senior utility Jordy Frahm.

Alexis Jensen, the freshman left hander, started on the mound for Nebraska.

Freshman outfielder Breanna Hanik singled up the middle on her first pitch of the game. Junior infielder Kaylie Walters attempted a bunt but was out, with Hanik moving up to second.

A fielding error from the Cornhuskers’ defense gave freshman infielder Allison Oneacre first base. That was all for the Nittany Lion offense as they left Hanik and Oneacre stranded on base.

Nemeth surrendered her first walk of the game in the top of the second, but was able to have another quick inning as she got her third strikeout of the game to keep the scoreboard clean, 0-0 going in the bottom of the second.

Jensen gave up a walk, giving sophomore catcher Macy Chamberlin first base, but that was all for the Penn State offense as Jensen was able to secure three strikeouts in the bottom of the second.

A walk by Nemeth gave senior infielder Lauren Camenzind first base, and Frahm was able to reach first off a defensive error from the blue and white. Another walk got three Nebraska runners on base with only one out in the top of the third.

Oneacre was able to secure the second out of the inning with a pop up, but a wild pitch brought Camenzind home to tally the first run of the game. A fly out ended the inning and the Cornhuskers momentum.

Nebraska led 1-0 going into the bottom of the third, where the top of the Penn State order was due. Hanik fouled out, and on Walters first pitch at the plate she hit a double that just grasped the foul line.

Oneacre launched the ball out of Beard Field, grabbing the lead with a two run homer. Penn State led 2-1 in the bottom of the third with just one out.

Junior utility Brooke Klosowicz tried to get the second home run of the game, but was unsuccessful as a Nebraska outfielder was able to catch the pop up.

Nemeth made an appearance in the batters box and was able to get a hit, but the Huskers defense was able to get the final out of the inning. The blue and white led 2-1 entering the fourth.

Two runners were left on base for Nebraska as Nemeth kept the board clean by securing two more strikeouts.

Chamberlain started things off with a single down the left side. Freshman Kali Kawowski entered the game as a pitch runner, and attempted to steal second, but the Huskers defense was able to catch her attempt..

Freshman infielder Gabby Gradishar won the battle against Jensen, advancing to first after being walked. Junior outfielder Frankee Flesher continued the momentum for the blue and white with a single through the right. Gradishar at second and Flesher at first.

Hanik stepped into the batters box and hit a single to left fielding bringing in Gradishar to increase the lead to 3-1. Junior Jordan George entered the game but was unsuccessful, striking out and leaving two Nittany Lions on base.

The Penn State outfield had a 1,2,3 inning as the defense stepped in the top of the fifth.

With two outs on the bottom of the fifth, Junior infielder Michela Barbanente crushed the ball out of the park for a solo home run. Extending the blue and white’s lead 4-1. That was all for the Penn State offense in the bottom of the fifth.

The Nebraska offense woke up in the top of the sixth, getting bases loaded with just one out, and a single from Kacie Hoffman got two Huskers home. Despite the two runs, the blue and white still lead 4-3 in the sixth.

Freshman right hander Mackenize Duncan was called up from the bullpen to close out the inning. Duncan had two runners on with only one out, and surrendered her first walk of the game with bases loaded.

Frahm continued the momentum with a single to right field, getting another Husker home to tie the game at 4-4.

A double got two more runners home, and Nebraska took the lead with only one out. Junior right-hander McKenna Young entered the game in relief for the Nittany Lions.

Penn State was able to close out the top of the sixth, but another run was scored for the Huskers as they took the lead 7-4 after scoring six runs in the sixth.

Frahm took over the circle in the bottom of the sixth. Getting her first strikeout against Sophomore Natalie Lieto. Gradishar was unsuccessful, getting struck out swinging and Flesher was grounded out. That was all for the blue and white in the bottom of the sixth.

Nebraska followed through with more momentum going into the seventh as another run was scored, extending their lead to 8-4. Camenzind came out for more as she launched a homer to left field, getting two more runs scored.

The blue and white were able to close out the top of the seventh, the Huskers scored three extending their lead 10-4.

In the bottom of the seventh the Penn State order was due, Hanik grounded out. Walters was struck out, and Oneacre looked to keep things alive with a single up the middle before Klosowicz grounded out.

Nebraska took the win 10-4.

title: Penn State track and field concludes outdoor regular season at Larry Ellis Invitational
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State wrapped up another record-breaking outdoor season with another weekend with more big performances.

The Nittany Lions competed at the Larry Ellis Invitational at Princeton on Friday and Saturday, earning 14 personal bests and five top-10 finishes in school history.

Friday

Freshman Matthew Sarnowski got things going on Friday in the men’s discus throw, where he earned a personal best for third place, throwing 51.55 meters.

Penn State earned its first shakeup in the record books in the women’s hammer throw, as senior Alex Pancoast threw a nearly five-meter personal best. Pancoast finished third with a throw of 60.09 meters — the sixth-best in Penn State history.

In the first event on the track, freshman Boh Ritchie set the No. 5 mark in school history in the women’s 800-meters, running 2:03.51 for third place.

In the men’s 800m, junior Kaleb Nastari finished fourth in 1:48.37, followed by teammate Carter Fitzgerald in fifth in 1:48.47.

Sophomore Kyle Moore continued to experience a breakout season in the men’s long jump, recording a personal best distance of 7.78 meters, good enough for second place at the meet and seventh-best in school history. Joe White finished just behind in fourth, jumping 7.21 meters for a season best.

Senior Lea Hatcher led five Nittany Lions in the women’s 1,500-meters, finishing third with a season-best 4:16.84. Katie Dallas finished in fifth with a personal best 4:19.99.

Despite multiple top-10 marks in school history earlier in the invitational, Allon Clay earned the first school record of the day in the Mike Brady Elite Men’s 1,500m. The Kanagawa, Japan, native ran 3:37.84 to win the race, breaking Brannon Kidder’s previous mark from 2014.

Senior Ryan Watson finished in third place in the 1,500m, running 3:39.38 to improve his top-10 mark to the sixth-best in school history.

In the men’s 3,000-meter steeplechase, senior Justin Healey capped off the final personal best on the day, running 8:52.09 for fifth place.

Saturday

Sophomore Hannah Riolo kicked things off on Saturday in the women’s high jump, where she earned a second-place finish with 1.79 meters.

The Nittany Lions got right back to their successful ways early in the competition as freshman Skye-Petrie Cameron threw the fifth-best shot put in Penn State history. Petrie-Cameron finished second in the event, throwing for 16.68 meters.

Junior Matt Dematteo hit the track for the men’s 400-meter hurdles, where he finished fourth overall in 52.21 seconds.

Sophomore Alexandria North wrapped up the day in the women’s 200-meters, where she finished second with a time of 24.22 seconds.

Up next

The Nittany Lions will compete at the Big Ten Championships in Lincoln, Nebraska, from May 15 to May 17.

title: Penn State baseball loses close series opener to Minnesota
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
After suffering another sweep last weekend to No. 19 Oregon, Penn State is still searching for its first series win of the season.

With the regular season winding down and their postseason chances dwindling, the Nittany Lions started a pivotal conference series at home against Minnesota on Saturday afternoon.

Penn State battled with Minnesota throughout the close series opener, but the Golden Gophers took the lead early and didn’t look back, winning 5-4 over the Nittany Lions.

The blue and white entered the contest second to last in the Big Ten at 12-29, while the Golden Gophers entered Happy Valley on a five-game winning streak and possessed a 27-17 record.

Coach Mike Gambino’s squad holds the worst ERA in the conference at 7.87, so limiting Minnesota’s bats was key to a series opener victory. However, the Golden Gophers struck first in the opening frame against Penn State’s ace Colin Fitzgerald.

The senior walked leadoff Jack Spanier on four pitches and then Davis Hamilton knocked an infield single to second. It didn’t take long for Minnesota to capitalize with two runners on and no outs as Weber Neels did damage. Neels launched a three-run blast to right center and the Golden Gophers were quickly up 3-0.

Fitzgerald soon found him in another jam shortly after Neels cleared the bases. Charlie Sutherland poked a single over to shortstop and Ty Allen followed by smashing a double into the left center gap. With runners in scoring position, Easton Richter drew a long walk to load the bases.

Though Minnesota had a golden opportunity to blow the game wide open, the pressure didn’t affect Fitzgerald. The Lititz, Pennsylvania, native mowed down three straight Golden Gophers on strikeouts. Ethan Cole, Jack Bello and Jack Mosh all struck out swinging to stop the bleeding as Fitzgerald escaped the first with only three runs plating.

Right-hander Isaac Morton swiftly retired the Nittany Lions in their half of the first, but Fitzgerald faced more pressure in the second. Hamilton drew a one-out walk and then stole second. Fitzgerald then threw a wild pitch with two outs and Hamilton advanced into third. Despite Fitzgerald walking Sutherland, he retired the next Golden Gophers and escaped the inning without any runs scored.

The Nittany Lions broke through later in the second frame. Morton lost control and hit catcher Avery Smith, but struck out right fielder Jesse Jaconski for the second out. However, center fielder Maddox McDonald delivered and tugged an RBI triple to the backstop in right to bring Penn State closer 3-1. Yet, Penn State couldn’t close the distance any further as shortstop Preston Yaucher struck out to end the rally.

Though Minnesota quickly extended its lead back to three in the third. Richter knocked a leadoff single to right and then Fitzgerald hit Bello a few batters later. Mosh singled to first and plated Richter to extend the Golden Gophers’ lead 4-1.

Despite Morton cruising through the blue and white’s lineup, right hander Mason Horwat provided a few quality innings of relief. The junior surrendered just two hits and totaled three strikeouts in his two scoreless frames.

After a few shutdown innings, Penn State cut into the three-run hole in the fifth. Yaucher grounded a routine out to Spanier at shortstop, but he fumbled the ball. Davis then drew a five-pitch walk and designated hitter Michael Anderson extended the rally with a single into center. Second baseman Bryce Molinaro lifted a sacrifice fly to right and scored Yaucher to cut Minnesota’s lead in half 4-2.

Bar a few base knocks in the top of the sixth, right-hander Mason Butash delivered a scoreless frame and the Nittany Lions inched closer. Right-hander Adam Urban retired the first two batters, but Jaconski started a rally and knocked a single through the left side.

A few batters later, Yaucher plated Jaconski with a single through the right side to bring the score to 4-3. Yet, the Golden Gophers took the run right back in the seventh with a leadoff double by Richter and Bello scored him off a sacrifice fly to put the Nittany Lions back down two runs, 5-3.

The back-and-forth swing continued into the bottom half of the seventh. Molinaro powered a solo shot to right center and brought Penn State back within one, 5-4.

Both squads were shut down the last few frames of the game as the Nittany Lions couldn’t mount the complete comeback and fell in the series opener.

Up Next

Penn State will face Minnesota for the second game of Saturday’s doubleheader approximately 35 minutes after the conclusion of the series opener.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: No. 15 Penn State men’s lacrosse takes down No. 7 Johns Hopkins to secure Big Ten championship
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
The last time Penn State played for a Big Ten championship, it was flat-out embarrassed, falling to Michigan 16-4 in Columbus, Ohio.

In their last meeting with Johns Hopkins, the Nittany Lions fumbled a five-goal lead in the third quarter by allowing seven consecutive goals, ultimately being defeated 11-10.

None of that history mattered on Saturday, though. The coming of the weekend brought with it a clean slate and a chance for the Nittany Lions to claim their first conference title since 2019, when they outlasted the Blue Jays in an 18-17 barn burner.

The blue and white bucked the trends, delivering an all-around offensive performance to take down Johns Hopkins, securing the championship with a 16-8 win.

Sloppy passing on the game’s opening possession saw the Blue Jays throw the ball out of bounds. Penn State capitalized, as Hunter Aquino caught a pass and cut between Johns Hopkins defenders before firing a shot past goaltender Oran Gelinas.

Hunter Chauvette, who posted a hat trick in the regular-season meeting, got the Blue Jays on the board with a strong cut and powerful sidearm shot into the Nittany Lions’ net.

With the Penn State midfield group battling injury, Ryan DiRocco saw the most significant action of his season. After the Chauvette goal, he notched just his second score of the year on a powerful wind-up shot from Gelinas’ right side.

A Jameson Smith goal not long after sent the game into a media timeout with the score knotted at 2-2. Johns Hopkins got another late goal in the quarter from Chuck Rawson to take their first lead of the contest, which they held until the first break.

The score was leveled with 12:18 to go until halftime, when Liam Matthews cut to catch a pass from Luke Walstrum and flicked the ball into the nylon. It wouldn’t stay that way for long, as Jimmy Ayers worked past his pole and dove on the Penn State crease to dunk one on the Nittany Lions’ goaltender Preston Hawkins.

After a huge check on the ride, Aquino won back possession. Penn State got a big goal when Chase Robertson threw a big hitch and bounced one past Gelinas to even the score at 4-4. Less than a minute later, Kyle Lehman notched his first goal of the tournament to put the blue and white into the lead.

As time bled off the shot clock, Matthews took a dodge on his defender and took a shot as he fell to the turf, the ball finding its way into the cage as the buzzer sounded. Penn State took a 6-4 lead into the half.

Matt Collison, the 2026 Big Ten Offensive Player of the Year, made his presence felt after a slow start with a quick goal to start the second half. After the goal, coach Jeff Tambroni argued with the referee, looking for a penalty that would’ve nullified the Collison score.

The strong opening to the half continued, as Chauvette shot between Peri DiBartolomeo and Alex Ross to sink one past Hawkins and level the score at 6-6.

After multiple Penn State shots found the pipes, Walstrum cut up the alley from behind the net and faked out Gelinas, finishing in close to regain the Nittany Lion lead.

Andrew Beard notched five points in the blue and white’s regular-season finale against Rutgers at SHI Stadium, and scored a highlight-reel goal to stretch the lead to two, stutter-stepping and driving towards the crease before tucking a close shot into the nylon.

Jack Iannantuono made it a four-goal run and three-goal lead for Penn State as he dove in front of Gelinas and flicked it in for the score. The newly-named Big Ten Freshman of the Year was shaken up on the play and headed to the bench after the score.

A Chase Robertson shot seemed to find the underside of the crossbar, but wasn’t ruled a goal on the field. Tambroni threw the red flag to challenge the play, and contrary to a few notable examples in recent history, the replay went Penn State’s way and was called a goal.

Aquino got his fourth point of the game with a slick shot into the top corner of the net, matching his jersey number with goal number 23 in his injury-marred sophomore campaign.

The Blue Jays finally ended a six-goal Penn State run with a shot from Chauvette that slipped under Hawkins to find the net.

Soon after the Chauvette goal, Aquino came up injured and hopped to the sideline without putting weight on his sleeve-covered right leg, assumedly the one responsible for his absence in the regular season.

Michael Faraone joined in on the scoring, firing a low shot into the net on the man-up opportunity.

Lehman got his second soon after a face dodge led to a strong opportunity to score on Gelinas, and got his hat trick when the goaltender couldn’t contain a rebound. The Ambler, Pennsylvania native scooped the loose ball and lobbed it into the open net to bring the Penn State advantage to 14-7.

John Jude Considine ripped a shot into the top-right corner of the net to deliver his first goal of the game and further extend the Nittany Lion lead.

Chauvette scored a rocket to cut the lead to seven. Tambroni emptied his bench, and Lindenwood transfer Wes Scurci worked his defender before firing a perfectly-placed shot into the top-left corner of the net, bringing the score to 16-8. The score would hold until the final buzzer sounded.

Seconds bled off the clock, and the Nittany Lion bench flooded onto the field to engulf Hawkins in jubilation after securing their first Big Ten championship since 2019.

Injury Report

Midfielders Patrick Carragher and Mac Hobbs were sidelined with undisclosed injuries. Jack Iannantuono was shaken up on his third-quarter goal, but returned to the game. Hunter Aquino came off the field in the fourth quarter and didn’t return to the game.

Up Next

With the win, Penn State secures an automatic bid to the NCAA Tournament. Analysts project the Nittany Lions to host a first-round game next weekend. The full bracket will be released at 9:30 p.m. Sunday.

title: Former Penn State men’s basketball commit Jamison White signs with Saint Louis
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
After decommitting from Penn State last month, Jamison White found a new team to join.

White has signed with Saint Louis, the team announced Saturday.

Signed ✍️Welcome Home, @jamowhite10!#RollBills pic.twitter.com/gF8olQytnx— Saint Louis Men's Basketball (@SaintLouisMBB) May 2, 2026

White was Penn State’s lone high school signee in the 2026 class back on National Signing Day in November. He committed to the Nittany Lions last September but reopened his recruitment on April 18.

White is a 247Sports composite 3-star in the 2026 class, ranking as the No. 241 player nationally, No. 41 power forward and the No. 2 player from Illinois.

The 6-foot-7, 215-pounder played his senior year at East St. Louis High School. He led his team to an Illinois Class 3A State Championship this season, averaging 19.7 points, 12.8 rebounds and 2.9 blocks per game, per MaxPreps.

White was named the Class 3A All-State team by the Illinois Basketball Coaches Association.

title: Penn State softball suffers loss to No. 3 Nebraska in series opener
author: Luci Palacios | For the Collegian
description:
After a road series sweep last weekend, Penn State returned to Happy Valley to take on No. 3 Nebraska, looking to carry its momentum into a tough matchup at home.

Penn State fell to Nebraska 5-1 to start the series.

Sophomore right-hander Abigail Britton started in the circle for the Nittany Lions. Nebraska’s Jordy Frahm started strong with a hit to right center and made it to second, but the next two batters got out.

Ava Kuszak took the plate and hit a line drive to center field, making it to first base. On a wild pitch, Frahm scored, giving the Cornhuskers a 1-0 lead.

Frahm took the circle for the Cornhuskers, and Breanna Hanik hit it to center for a lead-off single. Kaylie Walters hit a sacrifice bunt, and Hanik made it to second.

Already on a roll this season, Allison Oneacre continued it with a grounder to center field, which sent Hanik home to tie the game 1-1. It marked Oneacre’s 59th RBI of the year.

Frahm proceeded to halt the Nittany Lions' offense, striking out two to end the first.

After two outs to start the second, Kacie Hoffmann walked to give the Cornhuskers some momentum. Lauren Camenzind brought up the intensity with an RBI triple to right-center, giving Nebraska a 2-1 lead.

Coach Clarisa Crowell switched pitchers after going through the Nebraska batting order once. Junior McKenna Young got the nod to finish the second inning.

It was quiet for the blue and white when they took the plate and could not get anything going. Neither team could get anything going in the third, leaving the inning scoreless

This continued into the fourth as both teams put their dominant defenses on display.

Nebraska had other plans as Camenzind hit it to center field for a single. This snapped the streak of retiring eight consecutive Cornhuskers for the Nittany Lions.

Frahm stepped to the plate and laid down a sacrifice bunt, allowing Camenzind to reach second. Hannah Coor brought up the excitement with an RBI single, bringing the score to 3-1. That was all for the Cornhuskers in the fifth.

The fifth was quiet for the Nittany Lions as no runs were scored in the frame.

The Cornhuskers kept the momentum from the fifth as Jesse Farrell hit a solo home run, giving Nebraska a three-run lead, though that was all the Cornhuskers could get going in the sixth.

Nebraska put its dominant defense on display in the sixth. Frahm caused Walters to strike out, giving them the first out of the inning.

Oneacre grounded out to second, both her and Hanik failing to reach base, racking up the three outs. Nebraska did not plan to give out any more runs to the Nittany Lions. Another scoreless inning for the blue and white.

To start the seventh, Hoffmann led off with a double, displaying the dominance from both sides of the ball for Nebraska.

Coach Crowell switched pitchers for the last time, putting in McKenzie Duncan.

Pinch Runner Kenndai Williams swapped for Hoffman at second. Coor proceeded to hit it to the outfield and led Williams home for a 5-1 lead. The rest of the inning was quiet for the Cornhuskers.

The Nittany Lions went into the seventh hoping to turn the game around, but Klosowicz hit it to center field and got out. Barbanente followed up with the same fate.

Pinch-hitter Jordan George came in to keep the game alive for the blue and white, but it wasn't enough as the Nittany Lions fell to Nebraska in the first game of the series.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s volleyball knocked out of NCAA Tournament in five-set loss to UC Irvine
author: Chase Mitchell | The Daily Collegian
description:
Mark Pavlik’s final season leading Penn State has been a good one, as he brought his squad to the NCAA Tournament following a fourth consecutive EIVA Championship – the program’s 38th conference title – after finishing the season 22-7 and going undefeated in the conference through 12 matches.

However, there was still another milestone Pavlik and the blue and white were chasing: their first National Championship since 2008.

It just wasn’t written in the stars for the blue and white though, as Pavlik’s illustrious Penn State career was capped off with the Nittany Lions taking it to the wire but falling 3-2 to UC Irvine in the opening round of the NCAA Tournament.

The Anteaters got on the board first in the opening set with a William D’Arcy kill, but a following service error gave the blue and white its first point of the night.

Points were traded back and forth in the following minutes, but a 4-0 UC Irvine scoring run gave the Anteaters an early 10-5 lead, forcing Pavlik and the Nittany Lions into a timeout.

Former Anteater setter and current head coach David Kniffin’s squad tallied five kills en route to its early first set lead.

Five Penn State kills brought the blue and white closer, narrowing the gap to make it a 16-13 Anteater lead as Gaige Gabriel, Owen Rose and Matthew Luoma got their names in on the action.

Down six points with little momentum, Pavlik opted for Penn State’s second timeout of the opening set as outside hitter Sean Harvey looked out of his comfort zone with zero kills on five attacks for a -.600 hitting percentage.

Nothing changed down the stretch as the Anteaters overpowered the blue and white and cruised to a 25-18 opening set victory.

Again, the West Coast squad opened up the scoring in the second set with a Cameron Kosty service ace before Harvey finally got out of his slump, recording his first two kills of the night as a part of a 6-1 blue and white scoring run that gave it the early 6-3 lead before the Anteaters called its first timeout.

Another Harvey kill – his fourth of the night – extended Penn State’s lead to five points before UC Irvine turned on the jets and came storming back, cutting its deficit to 12-10 after another Kosty service ace which pushed the Nittany Lions into their first timeout of the second frame.

A 7-3 Anteater scoring run, backed by Andrej Jokanovic’s two service aces and a pair of Andreas Brinck kills, gave them their first lead since the beginning of the set as they jumped out to a 19-16 lead with the end of the second set in their sights.

Three consecutive blue and white kills made things interesting again as the scoring tied the set at 21-21 with outside hitter Bennett Wilson notching a pair of kills and Tristan Hassell getting in the mix as well.

The action came to a standstill when the score reached 25-25, but after some battling back-and-forth through some kills, blocks and errors, Penn State captured the second frame, winning 29-27 with Rose sealing it on his second service ace of the night.

Outside of his aces, Rose leads the nation in total blocks, entering Friday’s match with 152, leading to Penn State being the No. 1 blocking team in the nation.

It was an indecisive start to the third set, with UC Irvine taking an early lead before trading blows with the blue and white as six consecutive kills led to a 6-6 draw as Harvey moved up to 10 kills on the night while D’arcy improved his total to seven.

A third lead change gave the Nittany Lions the 11-10 lead as the Anteaters called for a timeout after back-to-back Wilson service aces marked the third and fourth of the set overall.

The Anteaters won a challenge for a block touch call that originally made it a 17-14 Penn State lead, but the reversal changed the score to 16-15 with UC Irvine facing just a one point deficit.

What started as utter domination for UC Irvine slowly turned into a match that looked like the Nittany Lions were in control as they extended their lead to 22-17 in the third frame after another Wilson service ace and more kills for Harvey and Hassell.

Hassell’s seventh kill of the night put a bow on the frame and Penn State, after winning the third set 25-19, was suddenly in prime position to pick up an opening round victory with one set to go.

With authority, the Anteaters got out to a commanding 6-2 lead in the fourth set in what felt like just a few seconds due to five Nittany Lion errors on their serves and attack efforts.

After an early Penn State timeout, UC Irvine extended its lead to 11-6 as D’arcy picked up his 11th kill of the night, hitting .455% along the way.

Just like the first set, the fourth was all UC Irvine from start to finish as it captured the frame 25-17, highlighted by Micah Goss picking up his 21st service ace of the season and Jokanovic notching his 11th kill of the night.

The Nittany Lions got out in front in the payoff fifth set, jumping out to a 3-1 lead after Hassell recorded a kill and a service ace on back-to-back plays.

After Kosty took a hit to the face, blood coming from his nose caused a lengthy delay as he was being attended to for a possible broken nose, with officials ordering him to wash his hands before being allowed back in the game.

With the lengthy medical delay in the books, three consecutive Anteater scores tied the action at 6-6, perhaps with the Nittany Lions being jarred from the pause in the action.

In the shortened fifth set, UC Irvine was the first squad to reach 10 points after a Harvey attack error with Goss and Kosty on the block assist.

A Trevor Clark kill gave the Anteaters a 13-12 lead as the action was coming to a head, with UC Irvine eying a hard-fought victory.

In the end, the Anteaters did just enough to hang on as a Goss kill sealed the deal to capture the fifth set 15-13, marking a 3-2 victory to advance to the regional final against UCLA and send Penn State packing.
